cards.front: What is swapping in operating systems?
cards.back: A swapping technique used by the operating system to temporarily store data in a faster storage device, such as RAM, while the main storage device, such as a hard drive, is being accessed.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of swapping?
cards.back: To improve system performance by reducing the time it takes to access data from slow storage devices, such as hard drives.

cards.front: What type of swapping is used when the system is low on RAM?
cards.back: Paging swapping, which involves dividing the memory into fixed-size blocks called pages and swapping out entire pages to disk when the system is low on RAM.

cards.front: What type of swapping involves swapping entire processes to disk?
cards.back: Segmentation swapping, which involves dividing the memory into smaller segments and swapping out entire processes to disk when the system is low on RAM.

cards.front: What is the advantage of swapping in operating systems?
cards.back: It allows the operating system to optimize system performance by controlling the amount of data in memory at any given time, reducing the need for physical storage devices.
